title: Agent and Patient identification in Tahitian
introduction: We will follow a simple path to see who does what to whom in Tahitian sentences. First, look at one-participant clauses (intransitives) to find the default subject position. Then compare two-participant clauses (transitives) to see how the doer (Agent) and the affected participant (Patient) are identified. In English, many readers expect subjects to be easy to spot and often to appear near the verb; here we will stress how Tahitian actually signals these roles. In Tahitian, clauses are predicate‑initial. With intransitives, the single subject comes right after the verb and has no case marker. With transitives, the Agent is also unmarked and follows the verb, while the Patient is typically introduced by the preposition **i** (for common nouns) or **ia / iā** (for personal pronouns and proper names). The verb shows no agreement with either participant. A productive passive suffix **‑hia** promotes the Patient to subject; if an Agent is expressed in a passive, it is introduced by **e** ‘by’. These facts together show a nominative–accusative pattern in the available data.

sections.focus: Intransitives: the default subject position (V S)

example.target_sentence: e haere 'ōrua i hea
example.description: One-participant clause. Verb + Subject order: subject **'ōrua** follows **e haere** with no case marker.

example.target_sentence: 'Ua topa 'o Hiro
example.description: One-participant clause. Subject follows the predicate and is unmarked; the proper name may appear with **'o** before it.

example.target_sentence: 'Ua rehurehu ta'u mata
example.description: One-participant clause. Subject **ta'u mata** ‘my eyes’ follows the verb and is unmarked.

example.target_sentence: Ua hōreo 'oia i mua i te ha'avā
example.source_sentence: He took an oath before the judge.
example.description: One-participant core with an oblique phrase marked by **i** (‘before the judge’). The subject **'oia** follows the predicate and is unmarked.

sections.description: In intransitives, the single subject comes right after the verb and bears no special marking. This baseline shows how subjects behave in Tahitian. Proper names may optionally take **'o** (e.g., **'o Hiro**).

sections.focus: Transitives (active): unmarked Agent; Patient with i/ia

example.target_sentence: 'ua 'ite a'e nei 'oe i te hōho'a o tō'u 'utuāfare
example.source_sentence: Have you ever seen pictures of my family?
example.description: V A O. Agent **'oe** is unmarked after the verb; Patient is a common-noun phrase with **i**: **i te hōho'a ...**.

example.target_sentence: 'ua mātau 'oe ia rāua
example.description: V A O. Personal Patient takes **ia**: **ia rāua** ‘them’. Agent **'oe** is unmarked.

example.target_sentence: e hōro'a mai 'ōrua i te tahi mā'a tupu nā mātou
example.description: Ditransitive active. Theme Patient with **i**: **i te tahi mā'a tupu**; Recipient with **nā**: **nā mātou**. Agent **'ōrua** is unmarked.

example.target_sentence: 'ua ho'o mai tā'u vahine i te moa i te mātete i te tahi mahana ra
example.source_sentence: My wife had bought a chicken at the market the other day
example.description: Transitive active. Agent **tā'u vahine** unmarked after the verb; Patient common noun with **i**: **i te moa**. Other obliques (place/time) also use **i**.

example.target_sentence: e mea ti'a 'ia fāri'i maita'i tātou ia rātou
example.source_sentence: We should welcome them in a friendly way
example.description: Transitive active. Personal Patient with **ia**: **ia rātou** ‘them’. Agent **tātou** is unmarked after the predicate.

sections.description: In active transitives, the Agent immediately follows the verb and is unmarked; the Patient is flagged: common-noun Patients take **i**, personal Patients take **ia / iā**. This is stable across different TAM values.

example.target_sentence: e hōro'a mai 'ōrua i te tahi mā'a tupu nā mātou
example.description: Theme Patient with **i**; Recipient with **nā mātou** ‘for/to us’.

example.target_sentence: 'Ua 'ōfati Teri'i i tāna faraoa nā māua
example.source_sentence: Teri'i broke his bread to share it between the two of us
example.description: Agent **Teri'i** unmarked; Theme Patient with **i**; Recipient marked by **nā māua** ‘for us two’.

example.target_sentence: Nā 'oe e fa'atae i tō mātou aroha iāna
example.description: Recipient is a personal pronoun marked by **ia** (**iāna** after vowels). The string **Nā 'oe e ...** highlights assignment ‘for you to ...’ and shows **ia** on the personal recipient.

sections.description: When a Recipient/beneficiary is mentioned, it is commonly introduced by **nā / nō**. The Theme Patient typically appears with **i**, and the Recipient with **nā / nō**. Personal recipients use **ia / iā**.

sections.focus: Passive voice: ‑hia on the verb; Agent with e ‘by’

example.target_sentence: 'ua pāhaehaehia e ana
example.source_sentence: But he has shredded it into pieces!
example.description: Passive verb with **‑hia**. Optional Agent of passive introduced by **e**: **e ana** ‘by him’. The promoted Patient is not introduced by **i**.

example.target_sentence: 'aita i 'itehia mai
example.description: Agentless passive: **'itehia** ‘be found’ with **‑hia**; no expressed Agent; Patient is promoted and not marked by **i**.

example.target_sentence: 'Ua 'ohihia te rau fara e te mau vahine
example.source_sentence: The pandanus leaves were collected by the women.
example.description: Passive with **‑hia**. Patient subject **te rau fara**; Agent of passive introduced by **e**: **e te mau vahine**.

example.target_sentence: 'Ua ravehia te tipi e tō'u pāpā
example.source_sentence: The knife was taken by my father.
example.description: Minimal passive counterpart of an active clause. **‑hia** on the verb; Agent of passive with **e**; Patient promoted (no **i**).

example.target_sentence: ua tīpe'ahia te mau ve'a
example.description: Passive with **‑hia**; no expressed Agent.

example.target_sentence: ua tāpū-'ē-hia tōna rima
example.description: Passive with **‑hia**; Patient promoted; no expressed Agent.

example.target_sentence: Ua fa'ata'erohia tā mātou 'ūrī
example.description: Passive with **‑hia**; Patient promoted; no expressed Agent.

example.target_sentence: Ua pe'e tāna tārahu
example.description: Passive‑like result: promoted Patient without **i**; no expressed Agent.

example.target_sentence: Ua rave-'ino-hia 'o Mere
example.description: Passive with **‑hia** on the verb; Patient promoted; no **i** before **'o Mere**.

example.target_sentence: A fa'afāura ri'i i te tāviri 'ia 'itehia e au
example.source_sentence: Make the key stick out a little so that I can find it.
example.description: Purpose clause with passive **'ia 'itehia** ‘so that it be found’, Agent of passive introduced by **e au** ‘by me’.

sections.description: The passive is formed with the suffix **‑hia**. The Patient becomes the subject (no **i**), and if an Agent is expressed, it is introduced by **e** ‘by’. Agent expression is optional, and many passives are agentless. Passive verbs take the same TAM particles as active verbs.

sections.focus: The particle i and the form ia: object marking and other functions

example.target_sentence: 'aita vau i 'ite
example.description: Negation pattern **'aita X i V**. Here **i** follows negation and introduces the verb (it is not the object marker in this slot).

example.target_sentence: 'ua ho'o mai tā'u vahine i te moa i te mātete i te tahi mahana ra
example.source_sentence: My wife had bought a chicken at the market the other day
example.description: The **i** before **te moa** marks the Patient; **i** also introduces location/time phrases (**i te mātete**, **i te tahi mahana ra**).

example.target_sentence: Ua hōreo 'oia i mua i te ha'avā
example.source_sentence: He took an oath before the judge.
example.description: Oblique phrase **i mua i te ha'avā** ‘before the judge’ introduced by **i** (not an object).

example.target_sentence: 'ua mātau 'oe ia rāua
example.description: Personal Patient marked by **ia**: **ia rāua**.

sections.description: The form **i** is multifunctional. In transitives it introduces common‑noun Patients, but it also marks times/locations and other obliques. After **'aita** in negation, **i** introduces the verb. Personal Patients use **ia** (often **iā** before vowels). Position and structure disambiguate these uses.

sections.focus: No agreement on the verb; stable across TAM and clause types

example.target_sentence: e haere 'ōrua i hea
example.description: Irrealis/future **e**: subject still unmarked and postverbal; no agreement on the verb.

example.target_sentence: 'ua 'ite a'e nei 'oe i te hōho'a o tō'u 'utuāfare
example.source_sentence: Have you ever seen pictures of my family?
example.description: Completive/past **'ua**: same Agent/Patient marking; no cross‑referencing on the verb.

example.target_sentence: Tē 'amu ra tō'u pāpā i te fāfaru
example.description: Progressive **tē ... ra**: Agent **tō'u pāpā** unmarked; Patient with **i**; no agreement on the verb.

example.target_sentence: 'aita vau i 'ite
example.description: Negation **'aita** with **i V**: Agent **vau** unmarked; no agreement morphology.

sections.description: Verbs do not cross‑reference Agents or Patients. Only TAM/negation particles occur (e.g., **'ua**, **e**, **tē … ra**, **'aita**). The way Agents and Patients are identified does not change with TAM or negation in these data.

sections.focus: Alignment and minimal pairs: nominative–accusative behavior

example.target_sentence: 'Ua rave 'o Hiro i te tipi
example.description: Active transitive: Agent **'o Hiro** unmarked (proper name may carry **'o**); Patient with **i**.

example.target_sentence: 'Ua ravehia te tipi e tō'u pāpā
example.source_sentence: The knife was taken by my father.
example.description: Passive counterpart: Patient promoted to subject (no **i**), Agent with **e**.

example.target_sentence: 'Ua topa 'o Hiro
example.description: Intransitive: single subject **'o Hiro** in the same postverbal slot as Agents of transitives.

example.target_sentence: Tē 'amu ra tō'u pāpā i te fāfaru
example.description: Active transitive: same subject position as intransitives; Patient marked by **i**.

example.target_sentence: 'Ua 'ohihia te rau fara e te mau vahine
example.source_sentence: The pandanus leaves were collected by the women.
example.description: Passive: Patient surfaces as subject; Agent expressed with **e**.

sections.description: S (intransitive subject) patterns like A (transitive Agent): both are unmarked and immediately postverbal. P (transitive Patient) is specially marked by **i/ia**, or becomes the subject in passives. These facts support a nominative–accusative alignment in the available data.

conclusion: What to keep in mind: Tahitian clauses are verb‑first. The intransitive subject and the transitive Agent both come right after the verb and are unmarked. The transitive Patient is typically introduced by **i** (common nouns) or **ia / iā** (personal/pronominal). The verb never agrees with Agent or Patient. Passive verbs use **‑hia**; the Patient becomes subject (no **i**) and any Agent is introduced by **e** ‘by’. The particle **i** is multifunctional (object, time, place, after **'aita**), so position and structure matter for interpretation. Overall, the alignment is nominative–accusative in these data.
